text: Hello, I'm sharing a low anterior resection with essentially a complete TME and a sewn coloanal anastomosis. This was actually for a recto sigmoid cancer in a woman with a very short retroperitoneal rectum. The tumor is actually here at the peritoneal or just above the peritoneal reflection and reviewing on the MRI, it was completely above the reflection and therefore the patient was selected for a surgical first approach.

The surgeon isolates the superior hemorrhoidal artery distal to the take-off of the left colic artery, while taking the inferior mesenteric branch adjacent to the inferior mesenteric artery.
The surgeon isolates the superior hemorrhoidal artery distal to the take-off of the left colic artery, and proceeds to take the inferior mesenteric branch adjacent to the inferior mesenteric artery.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection, carefully dropping the gonadal vessels off the mesocolon in a patient with a favorable body habitus characterized by thinness and minimal visceral fat.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection, carefully detaching the gonadal vessels from the mesocolon in a patient with a thin body habitus and minimal visceral fat.

The surgeon initiates the total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection posteriorly, progressing along the right side. The dissection is performed circumferentially, maintaining the dissection within the 'holy plane' to achieve an adequate distal margin.

The surgeon carefully unfolds the tissue on the left side, which is slightly folded due to previous gynecological surgery, and proceeds with dissection along the left-sided total mesorectal excision (TME) plane.
The surgeon carefully unfolds the tissue on the left side, which is slightly folded due to previous gynecological surgery, and proceeds with dissection along the left-sided TME plane.

The surgeon carefully dissects anteriorly, navigating around the deep cul-de-sac to avoid the vagina. The mesorectum and rectum beneath the peritoneal reflection are visibly short, measuring approximately three centimeters.

The surgeon identifies the tumor and ensures an adequate margin around it. The vagina is released from the mesorectum, completing the posterior dissection down to the levator floor. The surgeon works circumferentially, using the camera to gain a deeper view and avoid the vagina.

The surgeon reaches below the lower extent of the levators anteriorly, using a vessel sealer for hemostasis while avoiding burning the rectum. At the floor, the remaining tissue consists mainly of muscular attachments of the levators to the rectal wall. The proximal mesentery has been taken, and the surgeon prepares to staple proximally.

The surgeon reaches below the lower extent of the levators anteriorly, using a vessel sealer for hemostasis while avoiding burning the rectum. At the floor, the remaining mesorectum is minimal, consisting mainly of muscular attachments of the levators to the rectal wall. The proximal mesentery has been taken, and the surgeon prepares to staple proximally.

The surgeon assesses the perfusion of the tissue, which appears satisfactory, and proceeds to apply a surgical stapler to staple the proximal section. Subsequently, the surgeon applies another staple to the distal section.
The surgeon assesses the perfusion of the tissue, which appears adequate, and proceeds to staple the proximal and distal ends of the resected segment.

The surgeon performs a sewn side-to-end Baker anastomosis from the side of the proximal colon to the staple line on the rectum in a wide open female pelvis. The visualization is excellent, and there is no tension on the anastomosis. However, the corner of the proximal bowel appears slightly dusky, indicating potential concerns with blood supply.

The visualization is excellent, and there is no tension observed. However, the corner of the proximal bowel appears dusky, indicating potential issues with blood supply.

The surgeon observes a dusky appearance in a corner of the proximal bowel during the anastomosis. Indocyanine green (ICG) imaging is used to assess perfusion, revealing a lack of enhancement in the affected area, prompting a revision of the anastomosis.

The surgeon takes down the initial staple line and resects a few more centimeters to establish a new proximal margin. The surgeon then re-sutures the original posterior suture line, connecting the side of the colon to the end of the rectum.

The surgeon takes down the initial staple line and resects a few more centimeters of the colon to ensure an adequate proximal margin. The new proximal margin is established, and the surgeon proceeds to re-suture the original posterior suture line, connecting the side of the colon to the end of the rectum.
This patient had not received any new adjuvant treatment. I was not planning on diverting them. So I really wanted to make sure that I was very happy with my anastomosis.

The surgeon completes the posterior outer row of sutures and proceeds to make an enterotomy on the anterior rectum, cleaning out stool from the rectum. The hemorrhoids are visible through the opening of the anterior rectum. The surgeon then makes a proximal colotomy.

The surgeon completes the posterior outer row of sutures and proceeds to make an enterotomy on the anterior rectum. A small amount of stool is cleaned out from the rectum. The anastomosis is positioned at the level of the interrectal ring, with visible hemorrhoids through the anterior rectal opening. The surgeon then creates a proximal colotomy.

The surgeon begins sewing the inner posterior row of the anastomosis, using ICG fluorescence to assess the blood supply. The tissue appears healthier and more vibrant compared to previous observations.
The surgeon begins suturing the inner posterior row of the anastomosis and administers 2 mL of indocyanine green (ICG) to assess blood flow, expressing satisfaction with the improved perfusion.

The surgeon observes red bleeding, indicating good vascularization, while stabilizing the surgical platform and using reticulating instruments to facilitate the complex anastomosis. The surgeon completes sewing one side before proceeding to the anterior aspect, ensuring visibility of the posterior row.

The surgeon observes red bleeding, indicating good perfusion, while using reticulating instruments to sew the side of the anastomosis. The surgeon carefully places each suture to ensure visibility of the posterior row before proceeding to the anterior side.

The surgeon carefully places each suture in the desired location, transitioning to a canal to imbricate the inner and anterior row. The sutures are positioned to avoid meeting at the end, with the other suture being brought towards the middle. The anterior lip of the rectum is visible, showing the proximity to the perineal body with only about a centimeter to spare.

The surgeon transitions to a canal to imbricate the inner and anterior row, carefully placing sutures to avoid them meeting at the end. The anterior lip of the rectum is visible, showing the low position near the floor of the perineal body with minimal space to spare.
The surgeon ties the two sutures together and then uses the original posterior row suture to create a lumbering effect over the top of the anastomosis site.
The surgeon ties the two sutures together and then takes the original posterior row suture to loop over the top, securing the anastomosis.

This patient did very well. Actually had bowel function on day zero and was a post-update due discharge. I don't leave drains unless I had any concerns or certain circumstances and I did not create a loop ileostomy in this case. I do think that given how the level of the anastomosis and your comfort level, you could consider adding a loop ileostomy for this with an early closure. I do that selectively on a case-by-case basis, but I did not feel that it was necessary in this case as I was extremely happy with my anastomosis.

The surgeon completes the anastomosis and converts to laparoscopy for a leak test. The anastomosis is filled with gas, showing a widely patent and nicely distended structure without any visible bubbles, indicating no leaks.

The surgeon completes the anastomosis and converts to laparoscopy to perform a leak test. The bowel is filled and distended with gas, showing no bubbles, indicating a successful anastomosis. The operation concludes.
